content.subtitle: Uses
items: The future (for fixed events and schedules)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Water **boils** at one hundred degrees Celsius.
She **cycles** to the office every day.
My neighbour **works** as a software engineer.
He **does enjoy** a good meal after a long day at work.
The conference **starts** on the third of next month.
I'll send you the report as soon as I **finish** it.
Rodriguez **passes** to Vega — and Vega **shoots**! It's a goal!
CITY COUNCIL **APPROVES** NEW CYCLE LANE SCHEME
You **press** the button and **hold** it for three seconds.
So, a man **walks** into a library and **asks** for books about paranoia ...

content.subtitle: Emphatic Present Simple
content.description: We use do/does before the infinitive to add emphasis, often to correct a misunderstanding or express strong feeling.
examples: I know you think I don't care, but I **do care** about the environment.
She may not travel much, but she **does speak** three languages fluently.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions happening at the moment of speaking
Temporary situations and actions happening around now
Annoying or surprising habits (usually with always)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: The technician **is fixing** the printer right now.
We **are renting** a flat in the city centre while we look for somewhere to buy.
More and more companies **are switching** to renewable energy sources.
My colleague **is always forgetting** to reply to emails!
A woman walks into a shop. She **is wearing** a bright red coat and carrying a map ...
**Are you meeting** the new manager tomorrow morning?
I'll call you back once I **am sitting** down somewhere quiet.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Situations and states that started in the past and are still true now
A series of actions continuing up to now
Completed actions at an unspecified time in the past
Completed actions where the result matters now

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She**'s lived** in the same neighbourhood for over fifteen years.
The team**'s won** four matches and drawn two this season.
The engineers **have repaired** the fault, so the service is back to normal.
I**'ve just seen** an amazing documentary about deep-sea exploration.
Let me know as soon as you**'ve made** a decision.

content.subtitle: Note on American English
content.description: In American English, the past simple is often used instead of the present perfect simple.
examples: US: **Did** you **eat** yet? UK: **Have** you **eaten** yet?
US: I already **told** her. UK: I**'ve** already **told** her.

examples.note: In informal American English, gotten is sometimes used instead of got as a past participle when it means 'obtain', 'become' or 'move'.

examples: US: I **haven't gotten** your message yet. UK: I **haven't got** your message yet.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions and situations that started in the past and are still continuing (or have just stopped)
Emphasising how long an action has been going on

content.subtitle: Examples
items: I**'ve been waiting** for the delivery for almost two hours.
They**'ve been developing** the new app since the beginning of the year.
I won't feel confident presenting until I**'ve been practising** for a few more days.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: We usually use the present perfect simple to say how many times something has happened or how many things have been completed. We usually use the present perfect continuous to emphasise how long something has been happening.

examples.correct: She**'s sent** ten emails this morning. (number of completed actions)
She**'s been sending** emails all morning. (emphasising duration)

sections.title: Stative and Non-Stative Uses of Verbs

content.subtitle: Stative Verbs (generally used in simple tenses)
Watch out!
content.description: Many stative verbs can be used in continuous tenses when they describe an action rather than a state. Examples: be, consider, feel, have, look, see, smell, taste, think, weigh.

examples.correct: I **think** this app is very useful. (state: think = believe)
I**'m thinking** about upgrading my phone. (action: think = consider)
The chef **is smelling** the herbs to check they are fresh. (action)

sections.title: A: Present simple or continuous?
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: Look at those clouds! I think it **rains / is raining** quite heavily out there.
The sports centre **opens / is opening** at seven o'clock every morning.
We **don't usually hold / aren't usually holding** team meetings on Fridays.
My flatmate **always borrows / is always borrowing** my headphones without asking!
More and more travellers **choose / are choosing** trains over short-haul flights these days.
Could you call back later? I **have / am having** an online meeting at the moment.
The festival **takes / is taking** place on the last weekend of July every year.
Where **do you go / are you going** on holiday this summer? Have you booked anything?
Scientists **warn / are warning** that global temperatures **continue / are continuing** to rise.
The new café on the high street **does / is doing** really well, apparently.

sections.title: B: Error correction
sections.instructions: Each sentence contains a grammar error in the bold section. Write the correct form.

items.sentence: My brother **lose always** his keys — it's getting really annoying.
She **is never drinking** coffee after six in the evening.
The delivery **is arriving** every Tuesday morning without fail.
We **already finish** the first module of the course.
I'm afraid the director **is not knowing** the answer to your question.
How long **are you working** for this company? You must know it very well by now.
She **is studying** biology at university — that's her permanent subject.
They **are owning** three properties in different cities.

sections.title: C: Present perfect simple & continuous
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences using the correct form of the verb in brackets. Use the present perfect simple or present perfect continuous.

items.sentence: It's the first time (I / ever / visit) ...... this part of the country.
(you / ever / work) ...... in a team with people from different countries?
The students (prepare) ...... for the exam all week — they deserve a break.
How many languages (she / learn) ...... so far? I know she started a new one recently.
items.answer: has she learned / has she learnt

items.sentence: I (wait) ...... for this update for months — I'm so glad it's finally here.
We (not / decide) ...... where to hold the launch event yet.
(the engineers / test) ...... the new software all morning, but they still haven't finished.
That's the third time this week (he / forget) ...... to log out of the shared computer.
I can't believe (you / never / try) ...... sushi before! You should give it a go.
The temperature (drop) ...... steadily over the past few weeks.

sections.title: D: Adverbs of time
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap to complete the sentences.

items.sentence: Have you ...... been to a country where you couldn't speak the language at all?
I've ...... checked in — the boarding gate opens in about twenty minutes.
She's been working on this project ...... the beginning of the year.
We've been waiting here ...... almost an hour. Where is everyone?
Have you finished editing the report ......? The client is expecting it today.
I've ...... heard back from the agency — I'm waiting for a reply.
So far this year, the team has ...... won a single match.
This is the first time I've ...... presented to such a large audience.
She's ...... visited twelve countries and she's planning two more trips this year.
Have things improved ...... since we last spoke? I hope so.

sections.title: E: Multiple choice
sections.instructions: Choose the correct answer: A, B, C or D.

questions.sentence: She (1) ...... on a new research project at the moment, so she's very busy.
Water (2) ...... at a lower temperature at high altitude than it does at sea level.
The company (3) ...... its prices three times already this year.
I can smell something burning — I think the oven (4) ...... for too long.
The museum (5) ...... on Mondays, so we'll have to go another day.
Look — the sky (6) ...... darker. I think there's a storm coming.
I (7) ...... the document twice and I still don't understand what it means.
(8) ...... you ever ...... a complaint about poor service in a restaurant?
My grandfather (9) ...... chess every Sunday — it's his favourite hobby.
The development team (10) ...... a new feature all week and they're nearly done.

sections.title: F: Stative verbs (cloze)
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of a stative verb from the box. Use each verb once.

items.sentence: Everyone (1) ...... that regular exercise is good for your health.
This bag (2) ...... to my colleague — she left it here this morning.
I (3) ...... whether the project will be finished on time, given all the delays.
The sauce (4) ...... amazing — what spices did you add?
Each capsule (5) ...... a daily dose of vitamins B, C and D.
How much (6) ...... a return ticket to the capital ...... these days?
Most people (7) ...... online shopping to visiting a physical store nowadays.

sections.title: G: Matching (Stative verbs)
sections.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).

items.sentence: I think **B** the conference is going to be a great success.
items.answer: B – the conference is going to be a great success.

items.sentence: She resembles **H** exactly like his father — same eyes, same smile.
items.answer: H – exactly like his father — same eyes, same smile.

items.sentence: This report belongs **F** to the director, not to the marketing team.
items.answer: F – to the director, not to the marketing team.

items.sentence: The order contains **G** two bottles of sparkling and one still water.
items.answer: G – two bottles of sparkling and one still water.

items.sentence: I weigh **A** much more than I did before I started training.
items.answer: A – much more than I did before I started training.

items.sentence: The café smells **D** very strongly of fresh coffee in the morning.
items.answer: D – very strongly of fresh coffee in the morning.

items.sentence: The new youth centre matters **E** a lot to the local community.
items.answer: E – a lot to the local community.

items.sentence: I'm still considering **C** whether to accept the job offer.
items.answer: C – whether to accept the job offer.

sections.title: H: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Each sentence contains one extra word that should not be there. Click the extra word.

items.sentence: I've been to waiting for the bus for nearly half an hour.
She has been already finished the assignment before the deadline.
We have just been returned from a two-week trip to the coast.
Have you ever [have] tried cooking a meal from scratch without a recipe?
It is the very first time that I have ever spoken in public like this.
He is been studying for the exam all weekend and he looks exhausted.
The project has already been completed it ahead of schedule.
They still haven't yet decided on a venue for the annual dinner.
I know that she has been working very hard since from January.
We are living here ever since we moved from the countryside.

sections.title: I: Cloze
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap to complete the text.

items.sentence: Urban cycling (1) ...... grown enormously in popularity over the past decade.
City planners (2) ...... been adding new cycle lanes across the network since last year.
The number of commuters who choose to cycle to work (3) ...... risen every year.
Many people say they (4) ...... been cycling for years but never felt safe until now.
The local authority (5) ...... just launched a scheme to offer subsidised bike repairs.
Environmentalists argue that cycling (6) ...... one of the most sustainable ways to travel.
How long (7) ...... the city been investing in its cycling infrastructure?
So far, the new lanes (8) ...... already reduced congestion in the city centre.

sections.title: J: Key word transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first, using the word given. Do not change the word given. Use between two and five words.

items.sentence:
This is my first visit to the capital city.
**NEVER**
I ...... the capital city before.

She started learning to drive six months ago and she still hasn't stopped.
**FOR**
She ...... six months.
items.answer: has been learning to drive for

items.sentence:
The last time he went to the gym was three weeks ago.
**BEEN**
He ...... three weeks.
items.answer: hasn't been to the gym for

items.sentence:
They moved into their new flat and they are still living there.
**SINCE**
They ...... they moved in.

I find it annoying that my manager keeps interrupting me in meetings.
**ALWAYS**
My manager ...... me in meetings.

The last train for the city leaves at eleven forty-five.
**DEPARTS**
The last train for the city ...... at eleven forty-five.

This is the most challenging project I have worked on.
**EVER**
This is the most challenging project I ...... on.
